old_claim.note: Hospital Information Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from pacemakers_implantable to hospital_information_systems; HIS is monitoring and care-administration infrastructure, not part of the pacemaker device architecture.

source_shape.source_locator: VA history of implantable pacemaker development, pulse generator/electrode design, and patient implants.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes pacemakers through pulse generators, electrodes, implantation, and monitoring programs, without making hospital information systems part of the device.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This rejects HIS as a direct device prerequisite because the implantable pacemaker architecture and 1958-1960 history depend on electronics, power, and clinical implantation.

ontology_before: Pacemakers included hospital information systems as a direct prerequisite, mixing device architecture with later care-management infrastructure.
ontology_after: Pacemakers are modeled through electronics, portable batteries, and clinical therapy; hospital information systems remain separate monitoring infrastructure.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: hospital information systems no longer appear as a direct pacemaker prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The pacemaker node were re-scoped to a remote-monitoring or clinic-management platform.
A source showed the implanted pulse generator itself requires hospital information-system infrastructure.
Removing the edge also removed the clinical medicine context for implantation.
short_reason: Hospital information systems may monitor patients, but they are not a prerequisite for implantable pacemaker technology.
